title: Linked List - Insertion at k-th position

content.problemStatement:
In the last two lessons, we saw how to insert at the start and end of a linked list. How about inserting after some k-th node in the linked list?

The approach is similar to inserting at the end, but rather than going to the end of the list, we will just iterate to the k-th node.

To insert a node in between the k and k+1 node, we will have to change the next pointer of both the k-th node and new Node.


Set next of new Node to next of current
Set next of current to new Node
TaskComplete the function **insertAfterK** in IDE to insert an element after k-th node.


Input Format
First line denotes 'n' the number of elements in the list.

Second line consists of two space separated integers, xxx - the value to be added and kkk - the position after which the value is added.

Third line consists of n space-separated integers denoting the elements in the list.



Output Format
The complete list after insertion.
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- A line with integer n, the number of elements in the linked list.
- A line with two integers x and k, where x is the value to be inserted and k is the position after which the new node must be placed (1‑based, 1 ≤ k ≤ n).
- A line with n space‑separated integers representing the initial linked list.
All numbers are positive.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the elements of the linked list after the insertion, separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Three independent test cases. Each case inserts the given value after the specified k‑th node and prints the resulting list.

hidden.label: edge case – insert after first of two elements

solution.approach:
For each test case we build a singly linked list from the given values. To insert after the k‑th node we traverse the list k‑1 steps (starting from the head) to reach the k‑th node, then adjust pointers: newNode->next = current->next; current->next = newNode. Finally we traverse the list once more to print all values. The algorithm is linear in the size of the list.
Time Complexity per test case: O(n) – one pass to build the list, one pass to reach the k‑th node, and one pass to output.
Space Complexity per test case: O(n) for the linked list nodes.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) per test case (the linked list)
